## NetworkOptions
When `${Network}` token is used in the Text section, the BgInfo looks at every address on every network interface. It uses these filters to decide whether to include each address and network in the string it provides.


<!-- Implementation note: these tables should be generated by generate-documentation.ps1 and inserted at build time similar to toml-schema.md. This means the single origin of truth should be the json schema(s), which feed the documentation here, the inline documentation, the object models in the source code, and hence the commandline parser. -->

<!-- BEGIN:TOML_SCHEMA_SECTIONS -->
--8<-- "generated/network-sections.md"
<!-- END:TOML_SCHEMA_SECTIONS -->

### Filters
| Key | Data Type | If empty | If match | Fallback | Default | Descrption |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `RequireAdapterTypes`| `string[]` | Continue | Continue | Drop | `["Wireless80211", "Ethernet"]` | An array of adapter types to be included. [About network types](#about-network-interface-types). |
| `ExcludeAdapterTypes`| `string[]` | Continue | Drop | Continue | `[]` | An array of adapter types to be excluded.  If `IncludeAdapterTypes` is not empty, this rule will be Continueped. [About network types](#about-network-interface-types). |
| `RequireUp`| `boolean` | Continue | Continue | Drop | `true` | If set to true, interfaces that are down will be dropped. |
| `RequireFamily`| `string` | Continue | Continue | Drop | `IPv4`   | If set to `IPv4` or `IPv6`, addresses from other families will be dropped.
| `RequireMacAddresses`| `string[]` | Continue | Continue | Drop | `[]` | Interfaces with MAC addresses not appearing in the list will be dropped. |
| `IncludeSubnets`| `string[]` | Continue | Continue | Drop | `[]` | Addresses not matching one of the subnets in the list will be ignored. Subnets should be expressed using [CIDR notation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Classless_Inter-Domain_Routing#CIDR_notation), e.g. `192.168.1.0/24`.
| `IncludeNames` | `string[]` | Continue | Pass | Drop | `[]` | An array of [wildcard expressions](#about-wildcard-expression-matching). Adapters with a name matching any of these expressions will be passed immediately, otherwise they will be dropped.
| `IncludeDescription` | `string[]` | Continue | Pass | Drop | `[]` | An array of [wildcard expressions](#about-wildcard-expression-matching). Adapters with a description matching any of these expressions will be passed immediately, otherwise they will be dropped.


### Settings
| Key | Type | Default | Descrption |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `CidrNotation` | `boolean` | `true` | If true, IP addresses will be shown using . This shows how much of the address is part of the subnet prefix by suffixing a slash and a number of bytes.
| `IpAddressFormat` | `string` | <code>" ${Address}/${CidrBits} (${Origin})<br>"</code> | The format of the string that will represent a single IP address in the `${IpAddress}` substitution token, build using the [substitution tokens below](#substitution-tokens---per-ip-address) |
| `AdapterFormat`| `string` |  <code>"${AdapterName}<br>${MacAddress}<br>\${Speed}<br>\${IPAddresses}<br><br>"</code>|The format of the string that will represent a single adapter in the ['${Network}`](../toml-schema.md#substitution-tokens)substitution token, built using the the [substitution tokens below](#substitution-tokens---per-network-interface).

## Substitution tokens - per IP address
| Token | Description | Example |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `${CidrBits}` | The number of bits in the network suffix of the IP address. See [CIDR notation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Classless_Inter-Domain_Routing#CIDR_notation).  | `24`
| `${Origin}` | The origin of the IP address. See [about address origins](#about-address-origins).| `DHCP`, `Manual`, `Router advertisement-Random` |
| `${Address}` | The address in the standard string form - decimal for IPv4, hex for IPv6 | `198.51.100.14`, `fd6a:9a22:b579:8244:df24:fba6:eb71:2b09`

## Substitution tokens - per network interface

| Token | Description | Example |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `${Name}` | The name of the network interface | `Ethernet 6` |
| `${Description}` | The description of the network interface | `Realtek Gaming 2.5GbE Family Controller` |
| `${ID}` | A GUID representing the network adapter, generated by Windows when it's installed, and remaing consistent until you uninstall it. | `1BFD6D92-B38E-11EF-9376-00D861D55AAB` |
| `${Type}` | The network interface type expressed as a short string. [Details](#about-network-interface-types). | `propVirtual` |
| `${TypeLong}` | The network interface type expressed as a longer string from the [IANA IF Type enumeration](https://www.iana.org/assignments/ianaiftype-mib/ianaiftype-mib) | `proprietary virtual/internal` |
| `${Status}` | The operational status of the network interface. | `Up`, `Down` |
| `${Speed}` | The speed of the interface with units. The largest magnitude prefix yeilding a value >1 is selected. | `100Mb/s`, `2.5Gb/s 
| `${PhysicalAddress}` or `${MacAddress}` | The MAC address of the adapter | `02:DE:AD:BE:EF:00` |
| `${IpAddresses}` | A list of IP addresses assigned to the interface, optionally using CIDR notation, see `IpAddressSeparator` and `IpAddress` in [settings](#settings) | <code>" 198.51.100.14 (DHCP)<br> fd6a:9a22:b579:8244:df24:fba6:eb71:2b09 (Router-random)"</code> |

### About wildcard expression matching
Anywhere that filtering by string expressions is supported, the same rules are used. `*` matches any number of characters, `?` matches any single character and `\` escapes `*` or `?` so they can be used as literals.

<!-- Implementation note: this is achieved using https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/api/system.io.enumeration.filesystemname.matchessimpleexpression?view=net-10.0 -->

### About network interface types
Microsoft provides an [incomplete enumumeration](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/api/system.net.networkinformation.networkinterfacetype?view=net-10.0) of interface types in .NET. For example, type 53 is commonly used for VPNs and virtual network adapters, but this enumeration does not include an entry for that. To get the best of both, the `${Type}` token will fall back to short names provided in the [IANA IF Type enumeration](https://www.iana.org/assignments/ianaiftype-mib/ianaiftype-mib/) when it's missing from the Microsoft enumeration.  `${TypeLong}` always comes from the IANA list. `RequireAdapterTypes` and `ExcludeAdapterTypes` filters accept the .NET names, IANA short names, or integers.

<!-- Implementation note: BgRaster should build a FrozenDictionary for each direction to be used at runtime: 1 to look up the displayed IANA short and long names from the integer and one to look up the integer associated with each IANA short name and system.net.networkinformation.networkinterfacetype enum member string, created with case insensitivity. -->

### About address origins
IPv4 addresses are assigned to the adapter by one of these methods:
* Unknown
* Manual - manually configured on the host.
* Well known - specified by convention.
* DHCP - supplied by a DHCP server.
* Link layer - the address is derived from the physical address.
* Random - randomly assigned by the host.

IPv6 addresses are more complicated because the prefix (the network part) can be from a different origin than the suffix (host part):
* Unknown
* Manual - manually configured on the host. A manual suffix is vertually guaranteed.
* Well known
* DHCP - supplied by a DHCP server. A DHCP suffix if virtually guaranteed.
* Router - The prefix was supplied by the router

BgRaster treats IPv6 address origins as two parts, e.g. `Router advertisement-Random`. If the two parts are the same, they are collapsed into a single string, e.g. `DHCP`, `Manual`.

<!-- Implementation note: BgRaster should build a FrozenDictionary to allow fast lookup between the pairs of enum values in system.net.networkinformation.prefixorigin and system.net.networkinformation.suffixorigin and the required string, as detailed above. -->
